content:
7 min read
2 hours ago

--

After building the first version of FlightLog, I realized that the project had the potential to become more than a simple personal flight record app.
At first, FlightLog was designed to let users record flights they had taken. It supported basic flight information such as airline, flight number, aircraft type, registration number, departure airport, arrival airport, travel date, seat, and memo.
However, as I continued improving the project, I started to think more carefully about usability, data quality, statistics, and integration with my previous project, AeroType, an aircraft type dictionary web app.
This update focused on turning FlightLog from a basic record-keeping app into a more interactive aviation data system.
Why I Continued Improving FlightLog
The first version of FlightLog already had the basic structure I needed.
It could store flight records, display them as cards, search through them, and save the data in the browser using localStorage. It also included an aircraftTypeId field so that each flight record could eventually connect with AeroType.
But after testing the app more, I noticed a few areas that could be improved.
Adding a flight record should be easier.
Editing an existing record should be possible.
Seat information should be more detailed.
The app should help users understand their flight history through statistics.
Aircraft type links should work more reliably, even when the data is written in slightly different formats.
That became the direction of this update.
The goal was not just to add more features. The goal was to improve the way users enter, view, analyze, and connect aviation data.
Improving the Flight Record Form
One of the biggest improvements was the addition of an edit/update feature.
In the earlier version, users could add and delete flight records, but they could not easily modify an existing record. This made the app feel incomplete, because real personal data often needs to be corrected or updated later.
For this update, I reused the existing flight form modal and added an edit mode.
When the user clicks the edit button on a flight card, the modal changes from a new record form into an edit form. The title changes to “Edit Flight Record,” the submit button changes to “Update,” and the existing data from the selected flight card is automatically loaded into the form fields.
When the modal is closed or canceled, the editing target ID is reset. This keeps the logic safe and separates the create flow from the update flow.
This feature made the app feel much more like a real personal record management tool.
Adding More Detailed Seat Information
In the first version, the seat field was just a simple text input.
However, seat information can be more meaningful than just a seat number. A seat can also have a position and a cabin class.
So I expanded the seat data into three parts:

• Seat number

• Seat position

• Seat class
For example, a record can now show seat information like:
12A (Window, Business)I also added a small micro-interaction called a seat predictor.
When the user types a seat number, the app tries to guess the seat position automatically. For example, seats like A, F, or K are usually window seats, while seats like C, D, G, or H are often aisle seats.
The app automatically selects the predicted seat position, but the user can still change it manually.
This is a small feature, but it improves the user experience because the app helps the user complete the form faster.
Improving Date Input
Another usability improvement was the date input.
Using a native date picker is convenient in some situations, but typing dates directly can be uncomfortable depending on the browser or device.
To make the input more flexible, I changed the date field into a hybrid input system.
Users can type a date manually, and the app automatically inserts hyphens.
For example:
20260629becomes:
2026-06-29At the same time, users can still open a native calendar picker by clicking the calendar button.
This gives users both options: fast keyboard input and visual calendar selection.
Improving Real-Time Flight Data Quality
FlightLog also includes a feature that can use real-time flight information from an external aviation API.
While testing this feature, I noticed that API responses are not always clean or complete.
Sometimes, the first result in the response array does not contain aircraft type information or registration data. This can happen for reasons such as codeshare flights or incomplete data from the API provider.
To improve this, I changed the data selection logic.
Instead of simply using the first result, the app now searches the response array and tries to find the most complete record first. It gives priority to results that contain useful aircraft information, such as aircraft registration and aircraft type.
This taught me an important lesson: external API data should not be trusted blindly.
Even in a small personal project, data quality matters.
Korean Airline Name Mapping
The current version of FlightLog is developed in Korean, so I wanted the flight records to feel natural for Korean users.
However, aviation APIs often return airline names in English, such as:
Korean Air
Japan Airlines
Singapore AirlinesTo make the app more user-friendly, I added an airline name mapping table.
This table converts English airline names into Korean display names.
For example:
Korean Air → 대한항공
Japan Airlines → 일본항공
Singapore Airlines → 싱가포르항공This made the UI feel more consistent and natural.
It also reminded me that localization is not only about translating labels. Data itself sometimes needs to be transformed to match the user experience.
Sorting Flight Records by Travel Date
In the early version, flight records were displayed in the order they were added.
But for a flight log, that is not always the most useful order.
Users usually expect their newest flights to appear first, regardless of when they were entered into the app.
So I updated the rendering pipeline to sort flight records by travel date in descending order.
This means the latest flight is always shown at the top.
It is a simple change, but it makes the app feel more natural as a personal flight history tool.
The Interface: Boarding Pass Cards
For the visual design, I continued using a boarding pass-style card layout.
Each flight record is displayed like a small ticket. The card shows the airline, flight number, date, departure airport, arrival airport, aircraft type, registration number, seat information, and AeroType integration status.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

FlightLog displays each flight record as a boarding pass-style card. The current version is developed in Korean and includes route information, aircraft type, registration number, seat details, and AeroType integration status.
I like this interface because it makes the data feel connected to the aviation experience.
A flight record is not just a row in a table. It represents a trip, an aircraft, a route, and a memory.
The boarding pass layout makes each record easier to scan and more visually memorable.
Building a Statistics Dashboard Without Chart Libraries
Another major update was the statistics dashboard.
Instead of using an external chart library, I built the dashboard with Vanilla CSS and SVG.
The dashboard now provides several useful views of the flight history:

• Total number of flights

• Number of unique routes

• Top 3 most flown airlines

• Airbus vs Boeing ratio

• Seat preference ratio

• Top 5 most visited airports
The dashboard helped make FlightLog feel more like a data application instead of only a CRUD app.
Interactive Statistics Cards
I also made some of the statistics cards interactive.
For example, when the user clicks the airline count card, the app shows a popup with airlines grouped by route history. This makes it possible to see which airlines were used on which routes and how often.
When the user clicks the aircraft type count card, the app shows a list of aircraft types and flight counts.
Each aircraft type can also include a deep link to AeroType, allowing the user to move from the statistics dashboard directly to the aircraft dictionary.
This created a stronger connection between FlightLog and AeroType.
Strengthening AeroType Integration
One of the most important parts of this update was improving the connection between FlightLog and AeroType.
In the previous structure, each flight record had an aircraftTypeId, which was designed to connect with AeroType.
However, real data is often messy.
The same aircraft type can be written in different ways:
Dash 8 Q400
Dash-8
Q400
Bombardier Dash-8 Q400
Dash8-400Because of this, a simple exact match is not always enough.
I especially noticed this issue with the Bombardier Dash 8 Q400.
To fix this, I improved both sides of the integration.
On the FlightLog side, I added more aircraft code mappings and improved the aircraft type inference logic. If the app detects terms such as DASH-8 or Q400, it can convert them into the AeroType-compatible name.
On the AeroType side, I added a soft matching system for incoming deep link parameters. If an exact match fails, the app removes special characters and spaces, then tries to compare the normalized values again.
I also added a Dash 8-specific keyword detection filter so that older manually entered data can still open the correct AeroType aircraft page.
This was a small bug fix at first, but it became an important lesson in backward compatibility.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

FlightLog connects each flight record with AeroType through an aircraft type link. In this example, clicking Boeing 787–10 Dreamliner opens its corresponding aircraft dictionary page in AeroType.What I Learned
This update taught me that improving a project is not only about adding more visible features.
Some of the most important improvements happen inside the data flow.
I learned that user experience can be improved through small details, such as automatic seat prediction and date input formatting.
I learned that external API data needs validation, fallback logic, and sometimes transformation.
I learned that statistics can make a personal record app feel much more meaningful.
I also learned that linking two separate projects requires more than just passing an ID. Real-world data can have inconsistent formats, legacy values, missing fields, and edge cases.
From an Information Systems perspective, this was the most valuable part of the update.
FlightLog is not just a personal app anymore. It is becoming a small aviation data system that connects user records, aircraft type data, API data, statistics, and UI interactions.
